data.name: Classic Baked Clams Oreganata
data.description: Plump, juicy littleneck clams nestled in their half-shells under a golden, crispy crown of toasted breadcrumbs, aromatic garlic, fresh parsley, and bright oregano. Finished with a squeeze of fresh lemon for a vibrant bite.

refrigerator.notes: Best eaten immediately, but can be refrigerated overnight and reheated gently.

freezer.notes: Freezing changes the texture of cooked clams, making them rubbery.

storage.reheating: Reheat in a 180C oven until warmed through, about 5 to 8 minutes.

equipment.name: Clam knife
equipment.alternative: Steamer pot to gently pop open shells

instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 200°C/400°F. Line a baking sheet with crumpled foil or a layer of rock salt to stabilize the round clam shells so they do not tip over.

Using a clam knife, carefully open each live clam over a bowl to catch the briny liquor inside. Discard the top shell and run the knife under the meat to loosen it from the bottom shell. Place the half-shells on the prepared baking sheet.
tips: If shucking live clams is difficult, steam them in a covered pot with 60ml of water for just 2 to 3 minutes until they barely open, then pry apart and immediately chill.

instructions.text: In a mixing bowl, combine the breadcrumbs, minced garlic, chopped parsley, dried oregano, kosher salt, black pepper, and extra virgin olive oil. Stir well until the mixture resembles coarse, wet sand.
tips: Add 15ml of the reserved clam liquor to the breadcrumb mixture to amplify the oceanic flavor.

instructions.text: Spoon roughly a tablespoon of the breadcrumb mixture evenly over the exposed meat in each clam shell. Press the topping down lightly so it adheres to the clam and reaches the edges of the shell.
Bake the clams in the preheated oven at 200°C/400°F for 10 to 12 minutes. The breadcrumbs should be deeply golden and toasted, and the clam meat fully cooked through and firm.

doneness_cues.visual: Breadcrumbs are golden brown and crispy; clam juices are bubbling at the edges.
doneness_cues.tactile: Topping feels crusty and firm to the touch.

instructions.text: Remove the baking sheet from the oven and transfer the clams to a serving platter. Squeeze fresh lemon wedges over the hot clams immediately before serving.

troubleshooting.likely_cause: Clams were not properly purged of sand prior to cooking.
troubleshooting.prevention: Soak the live clams in a bowl of cold, heavily salted water for 30 to 60 minutes before shucking to encourage them to spit out sand.

troubleshooting.likely_cause: The clams were left in the oven too long or baked twice if pre-steamed.
troubleshooting.prevention: Bake only until the topping is golden and juices bubble. If pre-steaming to open the shells, reduce oven baking time by 2 to 3 minutes.

chef_notes: Always use freshly grated breadcrumbs or coarse panko for the best textural contrast against the soft clam.

If you are uncomfortable using a clam knife, steam the clams with a splash of water just until they pop open, then remove the top shell and proceed. Be mindful to reduce baking time slightly so they do not become overcooked.
Save the clam liquor! When shucking raw, catch the briny juices and mix a few teaspoons into your breadcrumb topping for an intense flavor boost without extra salt.
Ensure your live clams are fully closed before cooking. If a shell is open, tap it firmly; if it does not close on its own, discard it immediately.
Practice proper seafood handling by keeping raw clams chilled until the moment you shuck them, and clean all surfaces and clam knives thoroughly to prevent cross-contamination.
data.cultural_context: Baked clams, or Vongole Ripiene, are a staple of Italian-American seafood traditions, famously served on Christmas Eve as part of the Feast of the Seven Fishes. The classic Oreganata preparation specifically highlights oregano, olive oil, and garlic, celebrating the robust, sunny flavors of Southern Italy. It emerged as a resourceful way to elevate affordable local shellfish into an elegant, satisfying starter.
